name: Alecto
description: Alecto, whose name translates to 'unceasing' or 'implacable anger', is one of the Erinyes (Furies) in Greek mythology, goddesses of vengeance. Born from the blood of Uranus when Cronus castrated him, she, along with her sisters Megaera and Tisiphone, relentlessly pursued and punished those who committed crimes against natural order, particularly murder, impiety towards the gods, and offenses against family members. Alecto is depicted as a winged woman with snakes for hair, often carrying whips or torches to torment her victims. Her primary role was to inflict madness and suffering, driving individuals to despair and, ultimately, to justice or destruction. She is a personification of unrelenting rage and the inescapable consequences of wrongdoing. Alecto's wrath was feared by both mortals and gods, as her pursuit was relentless and her punishments severe. She embodies the dark side of justice, focusing on retribution rather than rehabilitation. Unlike some other deities who could be appeased, Alecto was notoriously difficult to placate, symbolizing the idea that some actions are unforgivable. She represents the inescapable consequences of evil deeds and the psychological torment that accompanies guilt and remorse. Alecto often worked in concert with her sisters, each specializing in a different aspect of vengeance, but she was particularly known for her ability to sow discord and incite violent conflict.
subtitle: Unceasing Wrath and Relentless Vengeance

keyMyths.title: Alecto and Aeneas
keyMyths.description: In Virgil's Aeneid, Juno enlists Alecto to incite war between the Trojans and the Latins. Alecto successfully stirs up Amata, the queen of Latium, and Turnus, the Rutulian king, leading to a brutal conflict.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of the Erinyes
keyMyths.description: Alecto, along with her sisters, was born from the blood that dripped onto the earth when Cronus castrated Uranus. This violent origin emphasizes their role as avengers of primordial crimes against the natural order.

keyMyths.title: Alecto's Pursuit of Orestes
keyMyths.description: Though primarily attributed to all the Erinyes, Alecto would have been involved in the pursuit of Orestes after he murdered his mother Clytemnestra. This highlights their role in punishing matricide and upholding familial justice.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A foundational text outlining the genealogy of the gods and the origins of the Erinyes.
sources.text: A foundational text outlining the genealogy of the gods and the origins of the Erinyes.

sources.author: Virgil
sources.description: Depicts Alecto's role in inciting war and chaos.
sources.text: Depicts Alecto's role in inciting war and chaos.

sources.author: Aeschylus
sources.description: Describes the Erinyes' pursuit of Orestes and their transformation into the Eumenides.
